purpose: Runtime cross-backend value-differential coverage ledger. ci/check_backend_parity.py is a SOURCE gate — it catches copy-paste divergence in the kernel text. It CANNOT see a value divergence: a GPU kernel that compiles fine but computes a DIFFERENT result than the CPU on the same input (a porting bug, a precision/carry difference, an endianness flip). The only thing that catches that is running the SAME input through CPU and GPU at runtime and asserting byte-identical outputs. This ledger lists each GPU-accelerated op that must have such a runtime CPU<->GPU value-differential probe, and (when covered) the audit module that performs it. Enforced by ci/check_backend_value_differential.py.
methodology: Feed identical inputs to the CPU reference and each GPU backend; assert byte-exact equality of the serialized outputs (signatures, points, field/scalar limbs, scan results). Any per-element mismatch is a backend value-divergence. The byte comparator is the verified detector; the LIVE run is advisory (needs a GPU — skipped on no-GPU CI runners).

ops.op: point/field/scalar arithmetic + ECDSA/Schnorr verify (GPU kernels vs CPU)
ops.claim: Every GPU algebraic kernel returns byte-identical results to the CPU reference on the same input (group law, GLV, multiscalar, verify).

ops.op: recoverable sign/verify, tamper detection, batch verify (cross-backend)
ops.claim: Recoverable-sig recid, tamper rejection and batch-verify verdicts agree across backends bit-for-bit.

ops.op: Bulletproof range-proof polynomial check (CPU prover -> GPU verify)
ops.claim: A valid CPU-produced range proof verifies on the GPU (verdict 1); a tampered scalar is rejected (verdict 0) — the GPU verdict matches the CPU verdict.

ops.op: BIP-352 silent-payment scan (GPU scan kernel vs CPU scanner)
ops.claim: GPU scan returns the same set of matched outputs and shared secrets as the CPU scanner on the same tweak/output set, byte-for-byte.

ops.op: GPU batch ECDSA/Schnorr signing (GPU kernel vs CPU CT signer)
ops.claim: For a fixed nonce-derivation input, GPU batch signing produces the same (r,s) / (R,s) as the CPU CT signer — exhaustive per-slot byte equality.
